perturbation.original_text: It is understood and agreed that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this letter agreement by either party or any of its Representatives and that the parties shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this letter agreement but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Vector understands and acknowledges that, except pursuant to a final definitive agreement regarding any transactions contemplated hereby, when, as and if executed, and subject to such limitations and restrictions as may be specified therein, (i) neither Captaris nor any of’ its Representatives make any representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Material, (ii) neither Captaris nor its Representatives shall have any liability to Vector or to any of its Representatives relating to or resulting from the use of the Evaluation Material or any errors therein or omissions therefrom, and (iii) only those representations or warranties which are made in any such final definitive agreement will have any legal effect. It is understood and agreed that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this letter agreement by either party or any of its Representatives and that the parties shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this letter agreement but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the equitable relief clause to the section disclaiming warranties and representations makes it less prominent and implies it only pertains to breaches related to the accuracy of the information presented. Equitable relief clauses are typically present near the end of a contract, in the 'General' or 'Remedies' section.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 3422 generally concerns the availability of injunctive relief. While specific performance isn't explicitly addressed in this section, the general principle that injunctions can be granted when monetary damages are inadequate as remedy, and that the availability of that relief are potentially compromised by moving the equitable relief clause to the representation and warranties section. While the agreement states remedies are not exclusive, the placement implies money damages would be the appropriate remedy for most sections outside of representation and warranties.
perturbation.location: Original section on general remedies; moved to the warranty disclaimer section.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3422. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Preventive Relief [3420 - 3424] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 3422. Except where otherwise provided by this Title, a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; 2. Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief; 3. Where the restraint is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings; or, 4. Where the obligation arises from a trust. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by moving the equitable relief clause into a section concerning representations and warranties. This placement implies that equitable relief is primarily applicable to breaches related to the accuracy of information, which is a misdirection. According to the perturbation explanation, equitable relief clauses are generally at the end of the contract. The original text makes no distinction about when equitable relief is appropriate. By contrast, the changed text implies it is only for breaches related to accuracy. Cal. Civ. Code § 3422 states that a final injunction may be granted where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. By restricting the application of the equitable relief clause, the modified text could be interpreted as limiting the availability of injunctive relief, potentially undermining the legal obligation to provide such relief when monetary damages are insufficient, creating a contradiction.
